items.html:
From one-size-fits-all to the individual

Not so long ago, a great deal of care in England was run for the convenience of the service rather than the person. In large institutions everyone rose at the same hour, ate the same meal and followed the same routine, whether it suited them or not. People were often grouped by their condition — ‘the residents’, ‘the diabetics’ — and their own history, preferences and personality faded into the background.

Person-centred care grew as a deliberate reaction against this. It asks a simple but powerful question: what matters to this individual, not just what is the matter with them? Care Certificate Standard 5 places that question at the heart of your role, so that support fits the person rather than forcing the person to fit the service.

Why it makes a real difference

Seeing someone as a whole individual is not just a nicety — it changes outcomes. When care reflects a person’s routines, relationships and wishes, they tend to feel calmer, keep their independence for longer and hold on to a stronger sense of who they are.

The opposite is also true. Care that ignores the individual can leave people bored, distressed or withdrawn, and it can make behaviour that services label ‘challenging’ far more likely. The Care Quality Commission expects providers to deliver care that is tailored to each person’s needs and preferences.

  • Identity: people stay connected to their past and their tastes.
  • Wellbeing: familiar routines reduce anxiety and confusion.
  • Trust: feeling known makes people more willing to accept support.

items.question: What best captures the shift from institutional care to person-centred care?

answers.text: Care is shaped around what matters to each individual, rather than the person fitting into a fixed routine.
answers.feedback: Correct. Person-centred care starts from the individual’s wishes and needs, not the convenience of the service.

answers.text: Everyone receives exactly the same routine, so nobody is ever treated differently.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Treating everyone identically is the institutional model person-centred care moved away from.

answers.text: The service decides the daily timetable and people adapt to it for efficiency.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Making people fit the service is the opposite of person-centred care.

answers.text: People are grouped by their medical condition so care is easier to organise.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Grouping people by condition overlooks the individual, which person-centred care sets out to avoid.

items.html:
The plan behind the care

A care and support plan is the written agreement that sets out how a person wishes to be supported day to day. Done well, it is not a form filed in a folder but a living picture of the individual: what they can do for themselves, where they want help, and how they like that help to be given.

The most important voice in the plan is the person’s own. Person-centred planning means the individual, and anyone they choose to involve, helps to shape their goals rather than having them decided for them. Your job is to read the plan, understand it and put it into practice, so that the care you give matches what was agreed rather than your own assumptions.

Build it, follow it, review it

A person-centred plan moves through a simple cycle that never really stops:

  • Build: gather the person’s history, strengths, needs and wishes, using their own words and involving those close to them.
  • Follow: deliver support exactly as agreed, and record clearly what you did and how the person responded.
  • Review: check regularly whether the plan still fits, because people, health and circumstances change.

Your daily notes feed this cycle. If you notice that something in the plan no longer works — a routine the person now dislikes, a goal they have met, a new difficulty — report it so the plan can be updated. Following an out-of-date plan is not person-centred, however carefully you do it.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about care and support planning.
items.textField: A care and support plan should be shaped by the *person* whose care it describes. Once it is agreed, your job is to *follow* it and record what you did. Because needs change, the plan must be *reviewed* regularly to check it still fits. If part of the plan no longer works, you should *report* it so that it can be updated. The plan works best when it is treated as a *living* document rather than a filed form.

items.html:
Start by assuming capacity

The Mental Capacity Act 2005 shapes how person-centred decisions are made in England. Its starting point is that every adult is assumed to have the capacity to make their own decisions, unless it is shown otherwise. You must never decide that someone lacks capacity simply because of their age, a diagnosis such as dementia, or a choice you happen to think is unwise.

Capacity is also decision-specific and can change over time. A person may be able to choose what to wear but need more support with a complex financial decision, and someone’s capacity may be better at one time of day than another. Before concluding that a person cannot make a particular decision, you must take all practicable steps to help them make it themselves.

Best interests and the least restrictive option

If a person genuinely cannot make a specific decision, even with support, then anything done for them must be in their best interests. That is not about what is easiest for staff; it means considering the person’s past and present wishes, feelings, beliefs and values, and consulting those who know them well.

  • Support first: give information in a way the person can understand — plain words, pictures, the right time and place.
  • Best interests: put the person’s wishes and wellbeing at the centre, not convenience.
  • Least restrictive: choose the option that limits the person’s rights and freedom as little as possible.

These principles keep even difficult decisions person-centred.

items.question: Mr Osei has dementia and wants to wear his favourite jumper, which staff think is too warm for the day. How should the Mental Capacity Act guide you?

answers.text: Assume he can make this everyday choice; a decision you find unwise is not proof that he lacks capacity.
answers.feedback: Correct. Capacity is assumed, and the right to make an unwise decision is protected by the Act.

answers.text: His dementia diagnosis means he lacks capacity, so staff should decide for him.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Capacity can never be assumed absent purely because of a diagnosis.

answers.text: Because the choice seems unwise, overrule it in his best interests.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Best interests only applies when a person lacks capacity for that decision; an unwise choice is not the same as lacking capacity.

answers.text: Decide for him quickly to save time on such a minor matter.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Even small everyday choices are the person’s own to make when they have capacity.

items.html:
Reading pain, discomfort and distress

Not everyone can tell you in words that they are in pain or feeling distressed. People living with dementia, a learning disability, or a condition that affects speech may show you instead. Because you know the individuals you support, you are often the first to notice when something is wrong.

Learn each person’s usual self, so that you can spot a change from it. Signs of pain or discomfort may include:

  • Facial signs: frowning, grimacing or wincing during movement.
  • Sounds: groaning, calling out or becoming unusually quiet.
  • Body language: guarding part of the body, tensing or restlessness.
  • Mood and behaviour: new agitation, withdrawal or refusing food.

Responding and adjusting the surroundings

When you notice pain or distress, respond promptly and in a person-centred way. Stay calm, use a gentle tone, and check the simple things first — is the person too hot or cold, uncomfortable, hungry, needing the toilet, or in surroundings that overwhelm them?

The immediate environment has a real effect on how someone feels. Small adjustments can ease distress before anything else is needed:

  • Reduce noise: turn down a blaring television or move to a quieter space.
  • Soften lighting: avoid glare and harsh shadows that can confuse or unsettle.
  • Comfort: adjust seating, temperature and clothing.

Always report signs of pain and the actions you took, so that the person’s needs are properly assessed and nothing is missed.

items.question: Because a person with advanced dementia may be unable to tell you in words that they are in pain, you should watch for changes such as grimacing, guarding part of the body or new agitation, and report what you see.
items.correctMsg: Correct. When someone cannot use words, non-verbal signs and a change from their usual self are key ways of recognising pain or distress.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Many people cannot report pain in words, so noticing and reporting non-verbal signs is an essential part of person-centred care.

items.html:
Keeping care person-centred together

Person-centred care is rarely delivered by one worker alone. Families and unpaid carers often hold years of knowledge about the person — their history, their likes, and the small things that soothe or upset them. Colleagues, nurses, GPs and other professionals each see a different part of the picture. Working well with all of them keeps care consistent and true to the individual.

The person always remains at the centre. Involve families in a way the person agrees to, share relevant information appropriately, and respect confidentiality. When everyone works from the same up-to-date plan and communicates openly, the individual receives joined-up support rather than a series of disconnected tasks.

A scenario: getting it right together

Doreen, who has moderate dementia, has become tearful at teatime and is eating very little. On a task-focused shift it would be easy to record ‘poor appetite’ and move on. Instead, you ask her daughter, who mentions that Doreen always ate her main meal at midday and found evening meals difficult, even years ago.

You raise this with your team and the plan is reviewed: Doreen’s cooked meal is moved to lunchtime, with a lighter supper in a calm, quiet room. Within a week she is eating better and is far more settled. Nothing here needed a specialist — just listening to family, sharing it with the team, and adjusting the plan around the individual.

items.question: In Doreen’s scenario, what made the response genuinely person-centred?

answers.text: Staff drew on the family’s knowledge, shared it with the team and adjusted the plan around Doreen’s long-standing routine.
answers.feedback: Correct. Listening to those who know the person, working as a team and updating the plan is person-centred care in action.

answers.text: Staff recorded ‘poor appetite’ and waited for a specialist to decide what to do.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Simply logging the problem and waiting misses the everyday knowledge that solved it.

answers.text: The daughter took over all decisions because she knows Doreen best.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Family knowledge is valuable, but the person stays at the centre; this was about listening, not handing over control.

answers.text: Doreen’s mealtimes were changed to fit the staff rota more easily.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The change was made around Doreen’s own routine and comfort, not staff convenience.
